title: US rare earths flow to Asia as domestic demand is slow to emerge
gist: US government-backed rare earth producers (MP Materials, Energy Fuels, Phoenix Tailings) are selling output to Japan and South Korea because domestic magnet-manufacturing demand hasn't materialized, exposing a structural gap: the US has funded upstream mining but lacks the downstream magnet-making capacity that only Japan and China have at scale.

insights.claim: US rare earths from Washington-backed MP Materials, Energy Fuels, and Phoenix Tailings are being sold to Japan and South Korea because US magnet-manufacturing demand has not yet materialized despite the Trump administration's supply-chain push.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Reveals that US government subsidies built upstream capacity without downstream offtake, a policy gap that keeps critical-mineral value flowing to Asia.
insights.quote: US rare earths produced by Washington-backed companies are flowing to Japan and South Korea, as American demand has yet to materialize

insights.claim: Only two countries produce neodymium iron boron magnets at scale—Japan, the originator, and China—leaving the US structurally dependent on Asian magnet-making even when it mines the raw material.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: The magnet bottleneck, not mining, is the true chokepoint; investors should watch downstream magnet capacity, not just ore.
insights.quote: Today, there are two countries where [neodymium iron boron] magnets are produced at scale. One is Japan, the originator, and one is China

insights.claim: Outside China, Japan produces 10,000-15,000 tonnes of NdFeB magnets per year, South Korea 2,000-3,000 tonnes, and the US just 1,000 tonnes or less.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Quantifies the order-of-magnitude US magnet-capacity deficit versus Asia, sizing the reshoring challenge.
insights.quote: Outside China, Japan produces 10,000-15,000 tonnes per year, while South Korea produces 2,000-3,000 tonnes annually, and the US produces 1,000 tonnes or less
insights.locator: China is the largest global producer

insights.claim: China's restriction of rare earth and critical-mineral access has become a US and Western national-security concern, with a dramatic cut in Chinese exports this year driving Japanese customers to clamor for US-produced metals.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Chinese export cuts are creating acute Asian demand that US producers are capturing, a near-term pricing tailwind for Western miners.
insights.quote: Japanese customers were "clamoring" for the rare earth metals it produces, given the dramatic cut in exports of the materials from China this year.

insights.claim: Phoenix Tailings' CEO warned that unless US defense primes move quickly, he will sell out to Korean and Japanese buyers who are paying top dollar faster.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: US defense supply chains risk losing domestic rare-earth output to higher-paying Asian buyers absent faster procurement.
insights.quote: Unless the [US defense] primes move quickly, I will sell out… other companies are paying top dollar faster.

insights.claim: MP Materials is the leading US rare earths producer by a wide margin, with NdPr oxide and metal sales primarily generated under its agreement with Sumitomo Corporation of Americas distributing to Japanese customers.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: MP's revenue is tied to Japanese offtake via Sumitomo, a key dependency for its top division.

insights.claim: MP Materials stopped selling to China's Shenghe Resources as part of its US government deal, and has signed agreements with General Motors and Apple to supply magnets, expecting to begin shipping finished magnets to GM this year.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: MP's pivot from Chinese buyer to GM/Apple magnet supply marks a real downstream US demand anchor investors can track.
insights.locator: In the same quarter a year ago

insights.claim: Energy Fuels won $725 million in conditional government funding in June, is acquiring Australian Strategic Materials (owner of a South Korean metal plant), and announced a $1.9 billion deal to buy German magnet maker Vacuumschmelze to route more product to VAC's US operations.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: M&A shows US producers buying downstream magnet capacity abroad to close the domestic manufacturing gap.

insights.claim: MP Materials' recent earnings have been boosted by a US government deal that guarantees a minimum sale price for some products and tops up any shortfall from third-party prices.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Government price-floor support materially derisks MP's revenue, a structural earnings backstop for the stock.
insights.quote: which guarantees a minimum sale price for some products and tops up any shortfall from the price paid by third parties.
insights.locator: MP's recent earnings have been boosted

insights.claim: Phoenix Tailings, backed by CIA-funded venture firm IQT, secured a conditional $500 million from Washington in June and said funding would help scale metal and oxide production to 'expand the pie for everyone.'
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Tracks the widening pool of federal capital flowing into US rare-earth startups, a sector-level subsidy tailwind.
